verses.text: Job has seen and heard all they say, and would rather 'reason with God' than argue with them. He turns from men to his Maker. When friends fail to satisfy, the soul does well to take its case directly to God.

Job has seen and heard all they say, and would rather 'reason with God' than argue with them. He turns from men to his Maker. When friends fail to satisfy, the soul does well to take its case directly to God.

Job has seen and heard all they say, and would rather 'reason with God' than argue with them. He turns from men to his Maker. When friends fail to satisfy, the soul does well to take its case directly to God.

'Ye are forgers of lies,' he tells them, 'physicians of no value'; would that they kept silent. Their words have only wounded. Sometimes the kindest thing a would-be comforter can do is hold his peace.

'Ye are forgers of lies,' he tells them, 'physicians of no value'; would that they kept silent. Their words have only wounded. Sometimes the kindest thing a would-be comforter can do is hold his peace.

Will they speak wickedly for God and show Him partiality, as if He needed their flattery? Job rebukes their twisting of truth to defend a theory. God is not honoured by arguments that misjudge the innocent in His name.

Will they speak wickedly for God and show Him partiality, as if He needed their flattery? Job rebukes their twisting of truth to defend a theory. God is not honoured by arguments that misjudge the innocent in His name.

Will they speak wickedly for God and show Him partiality, as if He needed their flattery? Job rebukes their twisting of truth to defend a theory. God is not honoured by arguments that misjudge the innocent in His name.

Would it go well if God searched them as they search Job? Their boasted maxims are 'ashes' and their defences 'of clay.' He turns their scrutiny back on themselves. Those quick to judge should ask how they would fare under the same gaze.

Then comes the famous cry of faith: 'though he slay me, yet will I trust in him,' for 'he also shall be my salvation.' Even believing God may kill him, Job will cling to Him. True faith trusts God in the dark, when all evidence seems against it.

Then comes the famous cry of faith: 'though he slay me, yet will I trust in him,' for 'he also shall be my salvation.' Even believing God may kill him, Job will cling to Him. True faith trusts God in the dark, when all evidence seems against it.

Job is sure that, given a hearing, he would be justified, and stakes his life on it. He longs to lay his ordered cause before God. A clear conscience gives a man boldness even when his sufferings seem to condemn him.

Job is sure that, given a hearing, he would be justified, and stakes his life on it. He longs to lay his ordered cause before God. A clear conscience gives a man boldness even when his sufferings seem to condemn him.

Job is sure that, given a hearing, he would be justified, and stakes his life on it. He longs to lay his ordered cause before God. A clear conscience gives a man boldness even when his sufferings seem to condemn him.

He asks God only two things: to lift the heavy hand and to free him from dread, then he will answer or speak. He pleads for a fair hearing without terror. The afflicted long not to escape God but to meet Him without overwhelming fear.

'How many are mine iniquities and sins?' he asks; why does God hide His face and treat him as an enemy, a driven leaf? He cannot grasp why God seems hostile. In trial, the believer's sorest grief is the sense that God has turned against him.

'How many are mine iniquities and sins?' he asks; why does God hide His face and treat him as an enemy, a driven leaf? He cannot grasp why God seems hostile. In trial, the believer's sorest grief is the sense that God has turned against him.

'How many are mine iniquities and sins?' he asks; why does God hide His face and treat him as an enemy, a driven leaf? He cannot grasp why God seems hostile. In trial, the believer's sorest grief is the sense that God has turned against him.

He feels God writes bitter things against him, making him bear the sins of his youth, while man rots like a worn garment. He aches under remembered guilt. Yet the very confession of old sin is the seed of the grace that will blot it out.
